title: भगवद्गीता अध्याय १६ - दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोग
titleEn: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 - Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga
devanagariText:
॥ अथ षोडशोऽध्यायः - दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोगः ॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
अभयं सत्त्वसंशुद्धिर्ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः।
दानं दमश्च यज्ञश्च स्वाध्यायस्तप आर्जवम्॥१॥

अहिंसा सत्यमक्रोधस्त्यागः शान्तिरपैशुनम्।
दया भूतेष्वलोलुप्त्वं मार्दवं ह्रीरचापलम्॥२॥

तेजः क्षमा धृतिः शौचमद्रोहो नातिमानिता।
भवन्ति सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातस्य भारत॥३॥

दम्भो दर्पोऽभिमानश्च क्रोधः पारुष्यमेव च।
अज्ञानं चाभिजातस्य पार्थ सम्पदमासुरीम्॥४॥

दैवी सम्पद्विमोक्षाय निबन्धायासुरी मता।
मा शुचः सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातोऽसि पाण्डव॥५॥

द्वौ भूतसर्गौ लोकेऽस्मिन्दैव आसुर एव च।
दैवो विस्तरशः प्रोक्त आसुरं पार्थ मे शृणु॥६॥

प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च जना न विदुरासुराः।
न शौचं नापि चाचारो न सत्यं तेषु विद्यते॥७॥

असत्यमप्रतिष्ठं ते जगदाहुरनीश्वरम्।
अपरस्परसम्भूतं किमन्यत्कामहैतुकम्॥८॥

एतां दृष्टिमवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानोऽल्पबुद्धयः।
प्रभवन्त्युग्रकर्माणः क्षयाय जगतोऽहिताः॥९॥

काममाश्रित्य दुष्पूरं दम्भमानमदान्विताः।
मोहाद्गृहीत्वासद्ग्राहान्प्रवर्तन्तेऽशुचिव्रताः॥१०॥

चिन्तामपरिमेयां च प्रलयान्तामुपाश्रिताः।
कामोपभोगपरमा एतावदिति निश्चिताः॥११॥

आशापाशशतैर्बद्धाः कामक्रोधपरायणाः।
ईहन्ते कामभोगार्थमन्यायेनार्थसञ्चयान्॥१२॥

इदमद्य मया लब्धमिमं प्राप्स्ये मनोरथम्।
इदमस्तीदमपि मे भविष्यति पुनर्धनम्॥१३॥

असौ मया हतः शत्रुर्हनिष्ये चापरानपि।
ईश्वरोऽहमहं भोगी सिद्धोऽहं बलवान्सुखी॥१४॥

आढ्योऽभिजनवानस्मि कोऽन्योऽस्ति सदृशो मया।
यक्ष्ये दास्यामि मोदिष्य इत्यज्ञानविमोहिताः॥१५॥

अनेकचित्तविभ्रान्ता मोहजालसमावृताः।
प्रसक्ताः कामभोगेषु पतन्ति नरकेऽशुचौ॥१६॥

आत्मसम्भाविताः स्तब्धा धनमानमदान्विताः।
यजन्ते नामयज्ञैस्ते दम्भेनाविधिपूर्वकम्॥१७॥

अहङ्कारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं च संश्रिताः।
मामात्मपरदेहेषु प्रद्विषन्तोऽभ्यसूयकाः॥१८॥

तानहं द्विषतः क्रूरान्संसारेषु नराधमान्।
क्षिपाम्यजस्रमशुभानासुरीष्वेव योनिषु॥१९॥

आसुरीं योनिमापन्ना मूढा जन्मनि जन्मनि।
मामप्राप्यैव कौन्तेय ततो यान्त्यधमां गतिम्॥२०॥

त्रिविधं नरकस्येदं द्वारं नाशनमात्मनः।
कामः क्रोधस्तथा लोभस्तस्मादेतत्त्रयं त्यजेत्॥२१॥

एतैर्विमुक्तः कौन्तेय तमोद्वारैस्त्रिभिर्नरः।
आचरत्यात्मनः श्रेयस्ततो याति परां गतिम्॥२२॥

यः शास्त्रविधिमुत्सृज्य वर्तते कामकारतः।
न स सिद्धिमवाप्नोति न सुखं न परां गतिम्॥२३॥

तस्माच्छास्त्रं प्रमाणं ते कार्याकार्यव्यवस्थितौ।
ज्ञात्वा शास्त्रविधानोक्तं कर्म कर्तुमिहार्हसि॥२४॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे
श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोगो नाम षोडशोऽध्यायः ॥
transliteration:
|| Atha Shodasho'dhyayah - Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yogah ||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Abhayam Sattva-Samshuddhir-Jnana-Yoga-Vyavasthitih.
Danam Damash-Cha Yajnash-Cha Svadhyayas-Tapa Arjavam.||1||

Ahimsa Satyam-Akrodhas-Tyagah Shantir-Apaishunam.
Daya Bhuteshv-Aloluptvam Mardavam Hrir-Achapalam.||2||

Tejah Kshama Dhritih Shaucham-Adroho Na-Atimanita.
Bhavanti Sampadam Daivim-Abhijatasya Bharata.||3||

Dambho Darpo'bhimanash-Cha Krodhah Parushyam-Eva Cha.
Ajnanam Cha-Abhijatasya Partha Sampadam-Asurim.||4||

Daivi Sampad-Vimokshaya Nibandhaya-Asuri Mata.
Ma Shuchah Sampadam Daivim-Abhijato'si Pandava.||5||

Trividham Narakasye-Dam Dvaram Nashanam-Atmanah.
Kamah Krodhas-Tatha Lobhas-Tasmad-Etat-Trayam Tyajet.||21||

Etair-Vimuktah Kaunteya Tamo-Dvarais-Tribhir-Narah.
Acharaty-Atmanah Shreyas-Tato Yati Param Gatim.||22||

Tasmach-Chhastram Pramanam Te Karya-Akarya-Vyavasthitau.
Jnatva Shastra-Vidhano-Uktam Karma Kartum-Iha-Arhasi.||24||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavad-Gitasu-Upanishatsu Brahma-Vidyayam Yoga-Shastre
Shri-Krishna-Arjuna-Samvade Daivasura-Sampad-Vibhaga-Yogo Nama Shodasho'dhyayah ||
hindiMeaning: षोडश अध्याय 'दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोग' में दैवी (दिव्य) और आसुरी (राक्षसी) स्वभाव का विस्तृत वर्णन है। दैवी सम्पदा — अभय, सत्त्वशुद्धि, ज्ञानयोग, दान, दम, यज्ञ, स्वाध्याय, तप, सरलता, अहिंसा, सत्य, अक्रोध, त्याग, शान्ति, दया, लज्जा, तेज, क्षमा, धैर्य, शौच — ये मोक्ष देने वाली हैं। आसुरी सम्पदा — दम्भ, दर्प, अभिमान, क्रोध, कठोरता, अज्ञान — ये बन्धन देने वाली हैं। आसुरी प्रवृत्ति के लोग कहते हैं — जगत में कोई ईश्वर नहीं, काम ही सब कुछ है। वे दम्भपूर्ण, अशुद्ध व्रत वाले होते हैं। काम, क्रोध और लोभ — ये तीन नरक के द्वार हैं, इन्हें त्यागना चाहिए। शास्त्र को प्रमाण मानकर कर्तव्य-अकर्तव्य का निर्णय करना चाहिए।
benefits: Clearly distinguishes divine and demonic qualities in human nature
Lists 26 divine qualities to cultivate for spiritual progress
Identifies lust, anger, and greed as the three gates to hell
Provides a moral compass for ethical living
Helps in self-assessment and character development
Encourages following scriptural guidance for right action
description: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 - Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga — भगवद्गीता अध्याय १६ - दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोग — is a sacred prayer offered to Lord Krishna. This revered text originates from Bhagavad Gita - Mahabharata. Devotees recite this prayer to provide a moral compass for ethical living and identify lust, anger, and greed as the three gates to hell. It is also said to list 26 divine qualities to cultivate for spiritual progress. This prayer is traditionally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami to invoke divine grace. Comprising 24 verses, it can be completed in approximately 12 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 - Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 — Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 (भगवद्गीता अध्याय १६), titled Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga (the Yoga of the Distinction between the Divine and Demonic Endowments), is the sixteenth chapter of the Srimad Bhagavad Gita and contains 24 verses. It systematically lists and contrasts the daivi sampada (divine endowment — the qualities of the godly) with the asuri sampada (demonic endowment — the qualities of those of demonic nature). Krishna teaches that all beings born in the world are of two kinds — daiva and asura — and details twenty-six divine virtues to be cultivated, then describes at length the psychology, conduct and tragic destiny of the asuric type. The chapter identifies kama (lust), krodha (anger) and lobha (greed) as the three gates to hell to be renounced, and concludes with the supreme injunction to take scripture (shastra) as the authority for what is to be done and not to be done. (Source: Srimad Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16; Mahabharata Bhishma Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the 26 divine qualities (daivi sampada) listed in Chapter 16?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 16.1-16.3 list the twenty-six daivi sampada qualities to be cultivated for liberation: (1) abhayam — fearlessness, (2) sattva-samshuddhih — purity of mind, (3) jnana-yoga-vyavasthitih — steadfastness in the yoga of knowledge, (4) danam — charity, (5) damah — sense-control, (6) yajnah — performance of sacrifice, (7) svadhyayah — study of scripture, (8) tapas — austerity, (9) arjavam — uprightness, (10) ahimsa — non-violence, (11) satyam — truthfulness, (12) akrodhah — absence of anger, (13) tyagah — renunciation, (14) shantih — peace, (15) apaishunam — absence of slander, (16) daya bhuteshu — compassion to all beings, (17) aloluptvam — non-greediness, (18) mardavam — gentleness, (19) hri — modesty, (20) achapalam — steadiness, (21) tejah — vigour, (22) kshama — forgiveness, (23) dhriti — fortitude, (24) shaucham — purity, (25) adroho — non-injury, (26) na atimanita — absence of excessive pride. Verse 16.5 adds: daivi sampad vimokshaya — the divine endowment leads to liberation; the demonic to bondage. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 16.1-16.5.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the asuri sampada (demonic qualities) described in Chapter 16?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 16.4 and 16.7-16.20 describe the asuri sampada in detail: (1) dambha — hypocrisy/ostentation, (2) darpa — arrogance, (3) abhimana — self-conceit, (4) krodha — anger, (5) parushyam — harshness, (6) ajnana — ignorance. The asuras do not know what is to be done and what is to be avoided; they have neither purity, nor right conduct, nor truth. They say the world is unreal, without basis, without God, with no cause beyond mutual lust. Holding this view, they perpetrate cruel deeds destructive of the world. Driven by insatiable kama, full of dambha and mada, they pursue impure intentions, taking impermanent pleasures as the supreme goal. Bound by hundreds of cords of expectation, dedicated to lust and anger, they amass wealth by unjust means for the gratification of cravings. They think themselves accomplished, the lord of all, the enjoyer, the perfect, the wealthy, the high-born — there is none equal to them. They are deluded, fallen, attached to false notions; they hate the Lord seated within their own bodies and within others. Krishna casts such hateful, cruel beings repeatedly into demonic wombs, where they sink lower and lower from birth to birth. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 16.4-16.20.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the three gates to hell mentioned in Chapter 16?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 16.21 contains one of the most quoted ethical declarations of the Gita: Tri-vidham narakasyedam dvaram nashanam atmanah, kamah krodhas-tatha lobhas tasmad-etat-trayam tyajet — Three are the gates of this hell, the destroyers of the self: kama (lust), krodha (anger), and lobha (greed); these three should therefore be abandoned. Verse 16.22 continues: A man liberated from these three gates of darkness practises what is good for the self and thereby attains the supreme goal. The triad of kama-krodha-lobha (also called the arishadvarga of six inner enemies when extended to include moha, mada, matsarya) is the central diagnosis of human bondage in the Gita and the entire Hindu ethical tradition. Bhakti saints, Vedanta acharyas, and modern teachers from Vivekananda to Gandhi quote this verse as the foundation of Hindu ethics. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 16.21-16.22.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Chapter 16 say about scripture (shastra) as the authority?
seoFaqs.answer: The chapter concludes with verses 16.23-16.24 which establish shastra (scripture) as the supreme authority for ethical decision-making: Yah shastra-vidhim utsrijya vartate kama-charatah, na sa siddhim avapnoti na sukham na param gatim — He who, having abandoned the injunctions of the scripture, acts under the impulse of desire — neither attains perfection, nor happiness, nor the supreme goal. Verse 16.24 declares: Tasmach-chhastram pramanam te karya-akarya-vyavasthitau, jnatva shastra-vidhanoktam karma kartum-iha-arhasi — Therefore, let the scripture be your authority in determining what should be done and what should not be done. Knowing what has been declared by the rules of the scripture, you should perform action here. This is the supreme prescription against rationalisation and self-justification — the asuric type discards scripture and follows desire; the divine type takes scripture as the lamp for action. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 16.23-16.24.)

seoFaqs.question: When is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Chapter 16 is recited on Day 16 of an 18-day Gita Parayana cycle. It is especially recited for ethical self-examination, discrimination between right and wrong conduct, victory over kama-krodha-lobha, and protection from asuric tendencies in oneself and the surrounding world. Specific occasions include: Wednesday (Budhvar — Krishna's day), Ekadashi, Gita Jayanti (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi), Janmashtami, Vijayadashami (Dussehra — celebrating the daiva-victory over asura-Ravana), and Holika Dahan (the burning of demonic Holika representing the burning of asuric tendencies). The chapter is also chanted as a daily ethical mirror — the 26 daivi qualities (verses 1-3) form a daily checklist for cultivation, and the asuric description (verses 7-20) is a diagnostic for tendencies to be uprooted. Modern Hindu households recite Chapter 16 to ward off the asuric currents of contemporary materialistic culture. (Sources: Gita Mahatmya; classical Hindu ethical tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Chapter 16 connect to the verse-by-verse Gita pages on this site?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 16 — Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga contains 24 verses, each studied verse-by-verse on this site at /gita/chapter-16/verse-1 through /gita/chapter-16/verse-24. The verse-level pages provide the Devanagari, transliteration, word-by-word meaning, English translation, Hindi anvaya and commentary by classical acharyas for each shloka — particularly important for the dense list of 26 daivi qualities (16.1-3) where each Sanskrit term packs a complete ethical concept, and for the long asuric-personality verses (16.7-20). This stotra page presents the entire chapter as a single text for parayana — recommended whenever the practitioner needs a moral re-orientation or the household needs purification from negative influences. The chapter pairs naturally with Chapter 17 (which extends the daivi-asuri analysis to faith, food, sacrifice, austerity and charity). (Source: Stotra and Gita parayana traditions.)
